calendar.matters:
ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Jan-08-2019 Off Calendar

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint
Off Calendar Dec-10-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Kimberly A. Fanady, Esq., Law Offices of Kimberly A. Fanady, Telephone 415-986-8467;
No appearance by plaintiffs counsel;

*** The Court previously adopted the tentative ruling and an order was signed. The tentative ruling was vacated because the Court received an email stating a personal emergency by plaintffs counsel and was not able to appear at the hearing. On December 14, 2018 the Court received an email from Plaintiffs cousnel agreeing to adopt the original tenative ruling. Order and Judgment are signed. **

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT PETER ZUCCARELLI, VACUUM COATING SUPPLIES LLC's Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint.

Defendants Peter Zuccarelli and Vacuum Coating Supplies, LLC's ("VCS") motion to quash is granted. After review of the supplemental papers filed by the parties, the court remains of the view that Shisler v. Sanfer Sports Cars, Inc. (2006) 146 Cal.App.4th 1254 controls this case and requires granting of defendants' motion. Plaintiff Oolong, LLC has the burden of demonstrating sufficient minimum contacts to warrant the exercise of general or specific jurisdiction. Oolong's evidence fails to show jurisdiction over VCS or Mr. Zuccarelli, who are both located in Texas. The evidence establishes that VCS passively advertised the Leybold Syrus 1105 machine for sale on e-Bay, Oolong reached out to VCS to purchase the machine, Oolong prepared its own purchase invoice and sent it to VCS in Texas, and the parties subsequently had communications regarding modifying the machine. There is no salient difference between this action and Shisler. When VCS initially posted the advertisement on e-Bay it was not directly targeted towards California. This case, like Shisler, involved a one-time transaction, albeit there were many communications about the transaction. The fact that the parties subsequently had numerous communications regarding the modifications to the machine does not change the character of the transaction from a one-off deal. In sum, Oolong has no evidence to establish general jurisdiction and fails to establish the "purposeful availment" prong of the test for specific jurisdiction.

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: William Trupek, Not Reported (302/HEK)
